mappings.rationale: Covers classical mechanics including Newton's laws, energy, momentum, and gravitational theory central to this section.
Directly covers Newton's law of universal gravitation, Kepler's laws, celestial mechanics, and gravitational mass discussed in this section.
Directly covers special relativistic mechanics, relativistic kinematics, and the equivalence of mass and energy discussed in this section.
Directly covers fluid statics, hydrodynamics, aerodynamics, laminar and turbulent flow, boundary layers, and compressible flow discussed in this section.
Covers the historical development of mechanics from Newton's laws through celestial mechanics to relativistic mechanics.
Directly relevant to Newton's laws of motion, law of universal gravitation, and the foundations of classical mechanics in this section.
Relevant to the foundational concepts of kinematics, projectile motion, and the law of inertia discussed in this section.
Covers elasticity, stress-strain relationships, yield strength, fracture, fatigue, and the mechanical properties of deformable bodies in this section.
Relevant to complex dynamical systems, nonlinear mechanics, and the sensitivity to initial conditions in mechanical systems.
Covers the mathematical formulation of mechanics including differential equations, Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics discussed in this section.
Covers fundamental concepts of matter including mass, energy, and the relationship between matter and mechanical forces.
Covers the philosophical foundations of space, time, motion, and the conceptual framework of classical and relativistic mechanics.
Relevant to celestial mechanics, gravitational interactions, and the two-body and n-body problems discussed in this section.
Covers rheological phenomena, deformation and flow, viscoelastic behaviour, and constitutive equations discussed in this section.
Relevant to conservation laws arising from symmetries in mechanics, including conservation of momentum and energy.
Relevant to the measurement of fundamental mechanical quantities including force, mass, velocity, and acceleration.
Covers structural mechanics, elasticity, fluid dynamics, and the practical application of mechanical principles.
Covers structural mechanics, stress dynamics of elastic materials, and the mechanical behaviour of deformable bodies.
Relevant to celestial mechanics including planetary orbits, the two-body problem, and gravitational perturbations.
Covers the development of classical mechanics by Galileo, Kepler, and Newton that forms the historical foundation of this section.
Relevant to the mathematical description of periodic motion, simple harmonic motion, and circular motion discussed in this section.
